title: Capital Investment, Equity Returns, and Aggregate Dynamics in Oligopolistic Production Economies
abstract: We analyze effects of tacit collusion in a dynamic general equilibrium model of oligopolistic sectors with capital investment and real frictions. Through their effects on equilibrium- and off-equilibrium stock prices, fundamental shocks affect incentives for defection from tacit collusion, amplifying the interaction between the real economy and financial markets as well as firms‚Äô risk exposure. The model implies ambiguous relationship between industry concentration and equity returns depending on operating leverage, which helps reconcile conflicting evidence in the literature. We find quantitative and empirical support for novel theoretical predictions regarding the effects of concentration on returns and investment.

Oligopolistic industries are ubiquitous and strategic behavior of firms in oligopolies receives long-standing interest.1In particular, the literature highlights the role of production capacity in protecting oligopoly profits through tacit collusion (Brock amd Scheinkman 1985;Benoit and Krishna 1987) and entry deterrence (Spence 1977;Dixit 1980). A related literature examines the effects of oligopolistic collusion on aggregate real outcomes (Rotemberg and Woodford 1992;Opp, Parlour, and Walden 2014) and risk factors (Dou, Ji, and Wu 2022). Meanwhile, another burgeoning literature considers the effects of industry concentration on equity returns. However, the dynamic general equilibrium effects of strategic oligopolistic behavior on firm-level capital investment, capacity utilization and equity returns, as well as on aggregate real and financial dynamics deserve further study.

The quantitative analysis of returns and aggregate dynamics with tacit collusion in production economies with investment remains open because the existing literature mainly examines oligopolistic outcomes in endowment economies (Dou, Ji, and Wu 2022) or in production economies without investment (Opp, Parlour, and Walden 2014). Meanwhile, empirical results on the relation of industry concentration and returns conflict: one strand of the literature documents a negative relationship (Hou and Robinson 2006;Gu 2016), but another strand presents a positive relationship (Bustamante and Donangelo 2017;Corhay, Kung, and Schmid 2020) or finds no significant relationship (Ali, Klasa, and Yeung 2013). Our paper theoretically, quantitatively and empirically helps address these issues.

We develop a dynamic general equilibrium model of a production economy with capital investment, composed of multiple oligopolistic consumer goods industries (or sectors) with unlevered firms. In concentrated sectors, tacit collusion occurs on product prices through strategic capacity utilization (production) and investment along subgame perfect equilibrium (SPE) paths, enforced by credible threats of ‚Äúpunishment‚ÄĚ through Bertrand-Nash price competition following defection. Analytically, the novel aspect of the model is the equilibrium characterization of firms‚Äô real decisions and equity returns, along with the resultant endogenous aggregate dynamics, through a dynamic optimization problem of maximizing equity values subject to atacit collusion incentive compatibility constraintthat current equity value gains from defection not exceed value losses from defection.

Focusing on oligopolistic strategic interaction with capital investment and real frictions provides novel insights on the interaction between the real economy and financial markets at both firm and aggregate levels. Fundamental (productivity) shocks affect incentives for defection from tacit collusion through their effects on equilibrium- and off-equilibrium stock prices. In response, firms adjust their real policies to maintain tacit collusion, thereby linking financial markets and real decisions. In general equilibrium, tacit collusion mediates the effects of sectoral shocks on endogenous aggregate dynamics in income, consumer price index, consumption and the risk-free rate. Indeed, shocks to low-concentration sectors affect tacit-collusive real and financial outcomes in concentrated sectors because ofintratemporalelasticity of substitution between industry products. Thus, while productivity shocks play a central role in the real business cycle and macrofinance models in competitive settings, these shocks drive micro and aggregate outcomes in oligopolistic production economies by affecting firm-level defection incentives channeled through equilibrium- and off-equilibrium equity prices.2

Moreover, firms‚Äô risk exposure to fundamental shocks is amplified by the possibility of defection from tacit collusion and off-equilibrium behavior plays a significant role in this exposure. Our analysis provides a novel perspective on the role of capital and real frictions‚ÄĒsuch as fixed and capital adjustment costs and endogenous depreciation‚ÄĒon priced risks. In the existing literature, real frictions amplify firms‚Äô risk exposure (from their capital) by restricting responses to fundamental shocks (Jermann 1998;Carlson, Fisher, and Giammarino 2004). Our framework highlights an additional channel: On- and off-equilibrium industry production capacities are central to tacit collusion because they determine short-term benefits and long-term expected costs of defection. Through their influence on both equilibrium and off-equilibrium capital investment, real frictions affect defection incentives and hence priced risks; the return-frictions relation is consequently more complex.

We quantitatively assess the effects of tacit collusion on firms‚Äô real-financial link and on their risk exposure. Similar to the literature (Baxter andCurcini 1993;Boldrin, Christiano, and Fisher 2001), we implement our quantitative analysis in a two-sector economy. In our case, these sectors comprise a concentrated sector with tacit collusion and a benchmark low-concentration sector where tacit collusion is not sustainable and there is Bertrand-Nash price competition. To isolate the effects of tacit collusion, we endow both sectors withidenticalproduction function and costs, real frictions, distributions of productivity shocks, and consumer utility weights. In this fashion, the sectors are constructed to be ex ante identical except for the differences in concentration and equilibrium strategies.

We find substantially higher volatilities of investment and output in the concentrated sector relative to the benchmark low-concentration sector. The equity risk premium and its volatility in the concentrated sector are also significantly greater than that in the low-concentration sector. Thus, our quantitative analysis shows that tacit collusion can significantly amplify both the link between financial markets and firms‚Äô real decisions as well as their risk exposure to fundamental shocks. We also provide graphical intuition for these results through impulse response functions to productivity shocks in the two sectors. In particular, this analysis clarifies the transmission channels from shocks to on- and off-equilibrium equity values to firms‚Äô strategic capacity utilization and capital investment decisions. Furthermore, the financial and real moments as well as the cyclical properties and model dynamics of investment and output with tacit collusion are generally closer to the data compared with the Bertrand-Nash equilibrium path.

We also quantitatively examine the micro and aggregate effects of variations in real frictions, specifically capital adjustment costs. The relation of adjustment costs and the equity risk premium in the concentrated sector is nonmonotonic, consistent with the theoretical implications of on- and off-equilibrium effects of real frictions outlined above. The presence of tacit collusion also affects the link between micro characteristics and aggregate dynamics. The existing literature generally finds that variations in micro-level adjustment costs have negligible effects on aggregate dynamics because of compensating changes in prices (Veracierto 2002;Khan and Thomas 2008). In contrast, we find significant aggregate effects of variations in firm-level adjustment costs and productivity processes with tacit-collusive prices. Finally, extensions of the quantitative analysis indicate that endogenous entry in the tacit-collusive sector amplifies mean and volatility of its risk premium, as well as the volatility of aggregate real and financial outcomes.

Another important implication of tacit collusion with capital and real frictions is that the relation of industry concentration and equity returns is generally ambiguous depending on the level ofoperating leverage, which helps explain the conflicting empirical results in the literature (mentioned above). Intuitively, variations in concentration have two opposing effects on markups and, hence, equity payoffs. There is an unambiguous negative ‚Äúmarket dilution‚ÄĚ effect of lower concentration because, for a given industry capacity, firms‚Äô profits are positively related to concentration. This dilution effect ceteris paribus leads to a positive relation of concentration and payoffs. On the other hand, lower industry concentration has a positive ‚Äúpunishment‚ÄĚ effect on equilibrium payoffs by facilitating a credible threat of substantially lower equity values following defections. We show that if the dilution effect dominates, then lower (higher) concentration ceteris paribus raises (reduces) the risk premium, and conversely when the punishment effect is relatively strong.

We build on this analysis to show that variations in operating leverage (in the tacit collusion framework) can help resolve the ambiguity in the concentration-return relation. Note how equity values of firms with high operating leverage (ie, with high fixed and/or adjustment costs) are relatively low during recessions, so that the punishment effect becomes weak for such firms in high stochastic discount factor (SDF) states. Hence, the relation of concentration and returns is negative for high operating leverage firms. An opposing argument applies for low operating leverage or high operating profitability firms and the concentration-returns relation should switch in sign for such firms. We quantitatively confirm these predictions in an industry equilibrium setting.

Moreover, higher depreciation costs of capacity utilization (Greeenwood, Hercowitz, and Huffman 1988;Jaimovich and Rebelo 2009) ceteris paribus lower optimal production and raise industry prices, weakening the punishment effect. Therefore, the concentration-returns relation is predicted to be more negative in industries with higher marginal depreciation costs of capacity utilization. Meanwhile, in terms of real variables, incumbents in high entry-threat oligopolistic industries with scale economies can deter entry through large capacities (Spence 1977;Dixit 1980), strengthening the relation of concentration and capital investment.

We also examine time variation in the effects of concentration on returns.Dou, Ji, and Wu (2022)finds that the conditional covariance between the SDF and aggregate returns rises in high aggregate return periods, suggesting that the concentration-returns relation will ceteris paribus be greater in high return states. Moreover, a growing literature shows that sales concentration and markups have increased in recent decades for a few ‚Äúsuper efficient‚ÄĚ firms (Berry, Gaynor, and Morton 2019;Autor et¬†al. 2020). Superior efficiency of larger incumbents reduces reliance on capital investment for entry deterrence in high entry-threat industries, weakening the link between concentration and investment.

Our empirical tests utilize theText-Based Network Industry Classification(TNIC) Herfindahl-Hirschman Index ofHoberg and Phillips (2016)as the concentration measure because the TNIC better matches our model. Our base sample includes all nonfinancial Compustat firms during 1989-2019 with available concentration measure. Our cross-sectional tests utilizeFama and MacBeth (1973)regressions on firms‚Äô stock returns, controlling forFama and French (1992,1993) factors and leverage. We find empirical support for the predicted effects of operating leverage on the concentration-returns relation, as well as the other above-mentioned predictions.

The interaction of real economy and financial markets attracts much attention (Morck, Shleifer, and Vishny 1990).3The existing literature highlights the real effects of financial markets based on the information role of security prices (Chen, Goldstein, and Jiang 2007;Bond, Edmans, and Goldstein 2012). We present a novel channel for real firm- and aggregate-level effects of both equilibrium and off-equilibrium stock prices in dynamic oligopolies that are widely prevalent.

A growing literature examines real and financial outcomes in industries with imperfect competition. Some papers in this literature‚ÄĒsuch as,Aguerrevere (2009),Morellec and Zhdanov (2019),Bustamante (2015), Farhi and Guorio (2018), andCorhay, Kung, and Schmid (2020)‚ÄĒconsider investment, but not the interaction of capital and real frictions with tacit collusion in a dynamic general equilibrium setting, as is the case with our study.Opp, Parlour, and Walden (2014)andDou, Ji, and Wu (2022)analyze the effects of tacit collusion in general equilibrium models with oligopolistic sectors. However,Opp, Parlour, and Walden (2014)consider a production economy with only labor input and hence do not consider the role of capital, whileDou, Ji, and Wu (2022)analyze an endowment economy model with exogenous aggregate output. Similarly, other papers in the literature, such asBustamante and Donangelo (2017), do not consider endogenous investment.Corhay, Kung, and Schmid (2020)positively link entry threat and risk premium in imperfectly competitive industries but do not consider the interaction of tacit collusion with capital and real frictions.

In contrast to this literature, we focus‚ÄĒtheoretically, quantitatively and empirically‚ÄĒon the effects of tacit collusion on firm- and aggregate-level real and financial variables in dynamic production economies with capital investment and real frictions. Our study makes two principal contributions: We show that the concentration-returns relation is generally ambiguous depending on the level of operating leverage, which reconciles conflicting evidence and generates novel results on the effects of concentration on returns and investment. Furthermore, we theoretically motivate and quantitatively verify that tacit collusion ceteris paribus amplifies firms‚Äô real-financial link and risk exposure to fundamental shocks. In particular, our analysis is novel in highlighting the importance of off-equilibrium capital and real frictions on firms‚Äô priced risks and real behavior, and the significant economy-wide effects of fundamental shocks through the tacit collusion channel in oligopolistic sectors. Thus, our model is distinct from other multisectoral models of production economies with capital investment in the literature that are placed in competitive settings (e.g.,Horvath 2000;Papanikolaou 2011).

The literature also considers effects of operating leverage (Lev 1974;Novy-Marx 2011) and operating profitability (Fama and French 2015) on the cross-section of returns, as well as the time-varying effects of market returns (Duffee 2005). In contrast, these firm-level and macrofinance characteristics play a prominent role in our analysis because they help resolve the theoretically ambiguous effect of industry concentration on equity returns. More generally, these conflicting effects point to the importance of capital and real frictions in understanding the concentration-returns relation.

The received dynamic oligopoly literature also focuses on symmetric SPE that maximize profits through worst-possible credible punishment following defection (Abreu 1986) and notes the theoretically ambiguous effect of concentration on markups (Brock amd Scheinkman 1985). In particular,Rotemberg and Woodford (1992)use this framework to analyze the effects of military spending shocks on economic activity in an oligopolistic production economy with tacit collusion. However, our analysis is distinct from this literature because of our focus on the interaction of tacit collusion, capital investment and real frictions with firm-level equity markets and aggregate income, consumer price index and risk-free rate. Our focus yields novel results on the interaction of financial markets with the real economy, as well as on the relation of industry concentration and equity returns.

Firms in each industry are unlevered and their equity shares are traded in frictionless security markets. Cash flows, determined by operating profits net of investment and fixed capacity costs, are distributed to shareholders as dividends. With an infinite horizon, firms can attempt to enforce tacit collusion on prices‚ÄĒthrough strategic capacity utilization‚ÄĒbased on credible retaliation threats. In particular, in the stochastic production and financial market setting of our model, defection incentives will evolve stochastically as firms evaluate their current profit gains from defection against loss of future equity value from retaliation. We now turn to characterize the equilibrium path.

2.1 Equilibrium characterization

The worst credible punishment path following a defection is the Bertrand-Nash (BN) SPE, where firms compete on prices subject to their capacity constraints. Consistent with the literature ( Abreu 1986 ; Rotemberg and Saloner 1986 ), and to generate empirical predictions, we focus on the symmetric optimal SPE that maximizes equity values of industry firms through tacit collusion enforced by threats of switching to the BN SPE following defection. Along the BN SPE, firms choose capacity utilization and investment policies ( u ~ t , I ~ t ) subject to the constraint of no gains from defection to a lower price. 9 Because of product homogeneity and symmetric cost structure, the BN SPE involves full capacity utilization ( Kreps and Scheinkman 1983 ). Comparing(18)and(19)and the BN equilibrium Eulerequation (12)above helps one clarify the ‚Äúnonstandard‚ÄĚ effects of real frictions on investment in the tacit collusion equilibrium: the ‚Äústandard effects‚ÄĚ are captured by terms in the first row of(19)while the effects due to tacit collusion are reflected in the presence of OEP investmentI~t+1and the TCIC-related termsŌátandŌát+1.

The tacit collusion channel suggests that firms‚Äô risk exposure due to fixed and adjustment costs, which is highlighted in the literature (Jermann 1998;Carlson, Fisher, and Giammarino 2004), will also be affected by the interaction of these real frictions with off-equilibrium-path behavior. For example, higher adjustment costs will ceteris paribus impede firms‚Äô capacity modification to address greater price competition in the BN equilibrium. Similarly, higher fixed costs raise value exposure to lower profits of the BN equilibrium. We will quantitatively examine below the effects of real frictions on firm-level equity risk premium and second moments of aggregate variables. Moreover, we will build on this intuition to derive predictions on the effects of operating leverage (driven by real frictions) on the relation of industry concentration and returns.

Now, the TCIC will be binding as long as the monopoly outcome is not incentive compatible at the firm level. However, in our model with a continuous state space, the monopoly outcome will not generically be incentive compatible for realistic number of industry firms even in concentrated oligopolies. In the other polar case, it can be shown‚ÄĒconsistent with intuition‚ÄĒthatut<1is not sustainable when the number of industry firms gets sufficiently large (seeInternet AppendixC3). Thus, the TCIC condition will generically apply for oligopolistic sectors except for low concentration levels; our analysis will henceforth focus on equilibrium paths with binding TCIC.

In sum, the equilibrium path real and financial outcomes(ut,It,St)are determined from three conditions:(1)the Eulerequation (18);(2)the (binding) TCIC(13); and(3)the asset market clearing conditionEt[ő©t,t+1Rt+1]=1,whereRt+1=(Dt+1+St+1)/St.But, as noted in the production-based asset pricing literature (Cochrane 1991;Liu, Whited, and Zhang 2009), in our setting there is an equivalence between equity returns (‚Ā†Rt+1)and investment returns (say,ROIt+1)because the firm‚Äôs real-side optimality conditions (see(18)) imply thatEt[ő©t,t+1ROIt+1]=1.10
article.3 Tacit Collusion, Real and Financial Li:
In our theoretical model developed above, stochastic fundamental (productivity) shocks affect incentives for defection from tacit collusion through their effects on equilibrium- and off-equilibrium stock prices, as quantified by the TCIC ((13) or(15)). In response, firms adjust their real policies to maintain tacit collusion, thereby creating a link between financial markets and real decisions of the firm. Thus, our theoretical analysis suggests that tacit collusion can ceteris paribusamplifyfirms‚Äô risk exposure as well as their real-financial link (so to speak) because of the effects of fundamental shocks on defection incentives. In this section, we quantitatively analyze these implications of our model by considering the equilibrium responses of salient financial and real variables to the fundamental sectoral productivity shocks{Ajt}j,t‚Ā†.11

Our quantitative analysis utilizes perturbation methods (Jin and Judd 2002) and undertakes quadratic approximations around the deterministic steady state of the model,12an approach widely adopted by the literature (Schmitt-Groh√© and Uribe 2004;Kim et¬†al. 2008). In particular, desirable properties of the accuracy of such approximations are documented in the literature (Caldera et¬†al. 2012;Fernandez-Villaverde, Rubio-Ramirez, and Schorfheide 2016;Andreasen, Fernandez-Villaverde, and Rubio-Ramirez 2017).13We also exploit the equivalence between equity and investment returns (noted above).

3.1 Calibration

To calibrate the model, we take industry-level data on capital, investment, output, and productivity from the NBER-CES manufacturing database. The latest data available are for 1958-2018 (annual). However, because productivity data are available only through 2016, our sample period is 1958-2016. Consistent with our framework, we focus on consumer goods industries. We obtain times-series data on capacity utilization in U.S. manufacturing industries and consumer goods industry production from the Federal Reserve Board (FRB). We extract information on aggregate variables that match quantities in our model from National Income and Product Accounts (NIPA). Specifically, we use real per capita personal consumption expenditures on durable and nondurable goods to quantifyC, and (nominal) per capita personal income from assets (or ‚Äúfinancial income‚ÄĚ) to calibrateW(see(2)). We obtain population data from the U.S. Census Bureau and the price deflator for personal consumption expenditures on goodsPfrom the Bureau of Economic Affairs (BEA). In addition, we obtain data on returns from Kenneth French‚Äôs website.

Similar to the literature (Baxter andCurcini 1993;Boldrin, Christiano, and Fisher 2001), we implement our model in a two-sector setting: a concentrated sector (indicated by sector 1) with tacit collusion and a low-concentration sector (indicated by sector 2) where tacit collusion is not sustainable and there is Bertrand-Nash price competition. We construct the concentrated (or tacit collusive) sector with manufacturing industries that are in the top quarter (25%) of sales concentration, defined as the industry sales share of the eight largest firms; correspondingly, the low-concentration sector (or BN equilibrium) is constructed by industries that are below the median sales concentration.

To isolate the effects of tacit collusion, we endow both sectors withidenticalproduction function and costs, real frictions, stochastic properties (namely, serial correlationŌĀjand volatilityőĽj)‚Ā†, and the utility weights for the two sectors in the CES basket. Thus, we make both sectors ex ante identical and then examine the equity risk premium and salient real moments in both sectors. The BN equilibrium outcomes serve as a useful benchmark for equilibrium outcomes in the tacit collusion sector. Moreover, the BN equilibrium path behavior provides intuition on the effects of productivity shocks on theoff-equilibriumbehavior of the tacit collusion sector.

We use a 1% annual discount rate, that is,őĪ=0.99,which yields a steady-state annual real risk-free rate of 1% which is close to the average real risk-free rate of 0.91% during 1960-2016. There is no consensus on parameterization of relative risk aversion and the IES in the literature. We use a risk aversion of 5, which is similar toJermann (1998), and is close to the midpoints of the range of risk aversion (2-10) considered reasonable byMehra and Prescott (1985), as well as the range of risk aversion estimates (2-7) in the finance literature (Elminejad, Havranek, and Irsova 2022). We set the IES (‚Ā†ő∑‚ąí1)at 1.9, which is consistent with estimates in the literature (e.g.,Attanasio and Vissing-Jorgensen 2003;Bansal, Gallant, and Tauchen 2010) and the parameterization in recent production-based asset pricing models (e.g.,Croce 2014). For complete symmetry, we give each sector the same utility weightŌēj(see the definition ofCtin Section 1.1) of 0.5. The choice of ES (‚Ā†ŌÉ),4.8, is consistent with the estimates for manufacturing goods inFeenstra (1994)andBroda and Weinstein (2006).

On the production side, we calibrate the common depreciation rate (‚Ā†őī)as 5%, which is close to the sample means ofinvestment rate(IR), or the investment-to-capital ratio, in the two sectors since theIRequals the depreciation rate in the steady state. This calibration is also consistent with the RBC literature (Gomme and Rupert 2007). Meanwhile, there is a wide variation in the literature regarding estimates of the capital adjustment cost parameterŌÜ‚Ā†, ranging from small to large values (Cooper and Haltiwanger 2006). We calibrateŌÜin both sectors at 8, consistent with the estimates inLiu, Whited, and Zhang (2009). The common autocorrelation coefficient of sectoral productivity shocksŌĀ(see(7)) is 0.94, which is consistent with estimates in the literature that log AR(1) productivity shocks are highly persistent in levels (Prescott 1986) and is close to its calibration in the production-based asset pricing literature (Jermann 1998;Kaltenbrunner and Lochstoer 2010).

The common marginal costs (h), fixed costs (m), production elasticity of capital (‚Ā†Ōą),and the common volatility of the (uncorrelated) sectoral productivity shocks (‚Ā†őĽ)are internally calibrated (or selected) to attempt and match the data for four model variables: the volatility of log changes in aggregate real per capita consumption; the volatility of log changes in the investment rate in the concentrated sector; the steady-state equilibrium capacity utilization in the concentrated sector; and the steady-state ratio of profit rates (ie, profit‚Äďto-capital ratios) between the concentrated and low-concentration sector. Our choice of parameterization is also consistent with significantly higher steady-state equilibrium dividends in the concentrated sector relative to the OEP (punishment equilibrium) dividends.14

Table¬†1summarizes the parameterization. The production elasticity of capital is consistent with strictly decreasing returns to scale. The assumed annual volatility of productivity shocks of 3% is within the range utilized (in annualized terms) in the literature (Gomme and Lkhagvasuren 2013;Bachmann, Caballero, and Engel 2013).15

Because both sectors are forced to be ex ante identical in terms of parameterization, we focus on the comparison of real and financial moments across the two sectors. Nevertheless, we provide the relevant moments in the data for reference. As noted above, the cost and productivity process parameterization is calibrated to match the volatility of (log changes) investment rate in the concentrated sector. The model generated volatility is 15.3%, which is close to the data (15.9%). However, with identical parameterization, the corresponding investment rate volatility in the low-concentration sector is 6.25%. Put differently, there is substantial amplification (close to 2.5 times) of investment volatility between the Bertrand-Nash and the tacit collusion equilibrium paths. There is an amplification of similar magnitude in the volatilities of percent changes in output across the two sectors:176.1% in the concentrated sector versus 2.5% in the low-concentration sector. Note that output volatility in our parsimonious model is constrained by the absence of labor and materials as production inputs. In sum, we find substantial amplification of the effects of productivity shocks on the volatility of investment and output due to tacit collusion. We also note that the moments in the concentrated sector are also closer to the sectoral data in comparison with the low-concentration sector.18

Turning to financial moments, with the parameterization at hand, the equity risk premium (ERP) in the low-concentration sector is effectively zero (up to two decimal points), while the ERP of concentrated sector is 0.71%. Thus, with ex ante identical sectors, there is an increase in ERP of about 0.7%. But since the benchmark ERP (in the BN equilibrium) is so low, theproportionalincrease of the risk premium due to tacit collusion is very large (over 200 times).19In incremental terms, the risk premium with tacit collusion is over 10% higher in terms of the low-concentration sector ERP in the data (6.9%). Furthermore, the volatility of ERP in the concentrated sector (2.9%) is substantially higher than the ERP volatility in the low-concentration sector (0.2%); the former is also proportionately closer to the data than the former. We thus find that tacit collusion significantly amplifies the mean and volatility of the ERP relative to the Bertrand-Nash equilibrium.

At the aggregate level, by internal calibration, the volatility of percent changes in aggregate real consumption (2.5%) is close to the data (2.6%). But the model understates the volatilities of percent changes in consumer price index (2.1% versus 2.9%) and (more significantly) financial income (3.4% versus 6.3%) in the data.20Meanwhile, the mean real risk-free rate is 1%, which is close to the 0.9% average real risk-free rate in our sample, in contrast to the well-known ‚Äúrisk-free rate puzzle‚ÄĚ (Weil 1989). That is, the model matches the volatilities of aggregate consumption growth and investment growth in the concentrated sector, as well as the mean real risk free rate. The volatility of the risk-free rate (0.9%) is lower than the corresponding volatility in the data (2.15%), however.

With the imposed restriction of identical parameterization across the two sectors, the mean and volatility of the ERP in both sectors are significantly lower compared with the data. In untabulated analysis, we utilize asymmetric parameterization that is informed by the relative volatilities of investment rates in the two sectors. With this parameterization and allowing entry and exit in the concentrated sector (seeInternet AppendixG), the model generates higher moments of ERP with tacit collusion as well as higher volatilities of aggregate financial income, price index, and real risk-free rate, that are closer to the data. In particular, the interaction of endogenous entry in the tacit-collusive sector and capital adds significant volatility to equilibrium real and financial outcomes.21

3.3 Tacit collusion, productivity shocks, and equilibrium responses

In this section, we develop intuition on the effects of tacit collusion on salient real and financial variables by utilizing impulse response functions (IRFs) to (one-standard-deviation) positive sectoral productivity shocks(őĶ1t+,őĶ2t+)(seeEquation (7)). We follow these responses over a 10-year horizon. These responses also determine, through the general equilibrium mechanism, the endogenous responses of aggregate variables in the model, namely,Wt,Pt,andCt,as well as the real risk-free rate (denoted byrf,t+1r).

Figures¬†1and2display the dynamics of equilibrium responses to (positive) productivity shocks in the concentrated and low-concentration sectors, respectively. Note thatőĶ1t+has an immediate positive effect on equilibrium equity priceS1tbut lowers the OEP equity priceS~1t.Intuitively, there is a decline in the OEP stock price because without tacit collusion (in the punishment equilibrium), higher productivity increases outputY1tfrom existing capacity (see(6)), thereby lowering the product price and expected profits (because of productivity persistence). Consequently, the TCIC is relaxed (see(15)) and equilibrium capacity utilizationu1tfalls toward the monopoly level, consistent with intuition onu1and tacit collusion provided in Section 2 In the parameterization at hand, this lower capacity utilization more than offsets the effects of higher productivity and raises theequilibriumpath product pricep1t.The role of tacit collusion in the real response to the positive productivity shock is illustrated by contrasting the effect ofőĶ2t+‚ÄĒthat is, a positive productivity shock in the low-concentration sector‚ÄĒinFigure¬†2. There, we see an immediate decline inp2tbecause there is no countervailing movement in capacity utilization due to the absence of tacit collusion. This channel, which links effects of productivity shocks on equilibrium and off-equilibrium stock prices to salient real variables like capacity utilization, is central to understanding the amplification of the real-financial link due to tacit collusion, quantified in Section 3.2 above.

Returning toFigure¬†1, because of product substitutability, the rise inp1t(in response toőĶ1t+)raises the demand for the product in the low-concentration sector, increasingp2t.Together, these higher prices result in higher aggregate price indexPtand dividend flow, or financial incomeWt,both of which have a mutually reinforcing positive relation with product prices in the economy (see(11)). Thus, we also find a positive general equilibrium effect ofőĶ1t+on the product price of the low-concentration sector. BecauseCt=WtPt,the net effect ofőĶ1t+on consumption is thus ambiguous. For the current parameterization, however, real consumption responds positively toőĶ1t+.In sum, productivity shocks in the concentrated sector are positively correlated with aggregate real consumption and financial income, as well as the aggregate consumer price index: These correlations are consistent with our data, using NIPA for aggregate quantities and NBER-CES database for concentrated sector productivity shocks.

We note also that the effects ofőĶ1t+on the aggregate variables immediately begin a reversion to their initial position. In particular, because of the reversion process, the expected growth rate of consumptionEt[ln‚Ā°(Ct+1Ct)]falls, which raises the expected SDF, other things being equal.

The impact ofőĶ1t+on the investment rate in the concentrated sector (IR1) is complex and subject to conflicting effects. First, there is a positiveefficiencyeffect: because productivity shocks are persistent, higher current productivity ceteris paribus increases expected marginal productivity of investment. Moreover, expected marginal value of investment rises because of the increased expected SDF. On the other hand, a downwardstrategiceffect on investment occurs because a positive productivity shock (‚Ā†őĶ1t+)lowers defection risk (by relaxing the TCIC, as noted above) and, hence, moves capacity toward the lower monopoly level. The net effect ofőĶ1t+onIR1is therefore theoretically ambiguous; but, for the current parameterization, investment rate in the concentrated sector falls. In contrast, there is no offsetting strategic effect on investment in the low-concentration sector, andFigure¬†2shows that a productivity shock in that sector unambiguously raisesIR2.22

Turning to financial variables, because of higher stock priceS1t,the expected (equity) rate of return in the concentrated sector (‚Ā†Et[r1t+1])falls. And the real risk-free raterf,t+1rfalls because of the higher expected SDF. Therefore, the risk premium in the concentrated sector is also lowered.

Figure¬†2shows the salient effects of positive productivity shocksőĶ2t+in the low-concentration sector. We highlight, first, the general equilibrium effects ofőĶ2t+on tacit collusion in the concentrated sector. There is an immediate downward impact onp2tbecause firms in that sector produce at full capacity. Hence, because of substitutability between products of the two sectors, the concentrated sector product pricep1tfalls. In contrast to the effect ofőĶ1t+inFigure¬†1, the TCIC now tightens, resulting in higher capacity utilization in the tacit collusion sector. Because of lower product prices, the aggregate price indexPtfalls, leading to an increase in real consumption that persists for two years. Hence, expected SDF falls in response toőĶ2t+because of higher expected consumption growth rate, resulting in lower stock prices in both sectors (‚Ā†S1t‚Ā†,S2t)and hence higher expected return in the concentrated sector. Because of the efficiency effect, investment in the low-concentration sector increases despite the lower expected SDF. Moreover, in response to lower expected SDF, the real risk-free rate rises, but the concentrated sector ERP still increases, while that in the low-concentration sector falls. Notably,Figure¬†2helps clarify the effects of productivity shocks when theconcentrated sectoris in the OEP punishment equilibrium and hence provides intuition on the effect ofőĶ1t+on the OEP equity priceS~1tobserved inFigure¬†1.

Overall, the IRF analysis above facilitates intuition on the quantitative effects (in Section 3.2) of tacit collusion and capital on firms‚Äô and financial markets‚Äô equilibrium responses to fundamental shocks. Consistent with the formal representation of defection incentives (see(13)), both on- and off-equilibrium responses of equity markets to shocks are central channels for firm- and aggregate-level real effects of shocks. Thus, firms‚Äô risk exposure and real responses to shocks are materially amplified through their (shocks‚Äô) effects on defection incentives. This not only affects firm-level risk premium and real volatility in the tacit collusion sector (compared to the benchmark sector), but is also consistent with the relatively low mean real risk-free rate in the model.

3.5 Effects of adjustment costs and productivity processes

The effects of real frictions, in particular capital adjustment costs, on firm-level equity risk premiums (e.g.,Jermann 1998) and aggregate real outcomes (e.g.,Veracierto 2002;Khan and Thomas 2008) receive substantial attention in the literature. Intuitively, adjustment costs should ceteris paribus increase firms‚Äô risk exposure to shocks by restricting their ability to adjust capital stocks to facilitate consumption smoothing by equity holders. However, as we discussed above (in Section 2.1), with tacit collusion, the equilibrium risk premium will be influenced by the effects of adjustment costs on the OEP punishment equilibrium. In particular, higher adjustment costs can ceteris paribus worsen OEP values through greater exposure to competitive pricing, thereby relaxing the TCIC and lowering risk exposure. The relation of adjustment costs and risk premium can therefore be theoretically ambiguous. Meanwhile, the macroeconomics literature generally finds that in competitive settings such frictions are essentially irrelevant for aggregate dynamics in general equilibrium because prices adjust to compensate for the frictions. But this conclusion may not necessarily apply with tacit collusive prices.

InTable¬†4, we examine the effects of adjustment costs on (sectoral) ERP and on aggregate real moments. To this end, we reduceŌÜfrom 8 (Table¬†1) to two lower values of 5 and 2. In conjunction with the analysis ofTable¬†2, we find that the ERP in the tacit collusion sector is nonmonotonic inŌÜ,first rising and then falling asŌÜis lowered, which supports the conceptual ambiguity in the adjustment costs-returns relation discussed above. The ERP in the low-concentration sector still remains effectively zero through the variation in adjustment costs. Furthermore, the effects of adjustment costs on the volatility of the ERP appear distinct between the tacit collusion and Bertrand-Nash sectors. Meanwhile, variations in firm-level capital adjustment costs clearly have significant effects on the aggregate real moments. In general, there is a monotone positive relationship between volatilities of aggregate real consumption, financial income, and price index. In sum, the analysis inTable¬†4supports the intuition developed above.

article.4 Industry Concentration and Equity Retu:
As mentioned before, the literature presents conflicting evidence on the cross-sectional relation of industry concentration and equity returns.23In this and the next section, we show‚ÄĒconceptually, quantitatively, and empirically‚ÄĒthat tacit collusion with endogenous capital and real frictions can help resolve the observed ambiguity in the concentration-return relation. In particular, our analysis highlights the role ofoperating leveragein helping resolve this ambiguity.

The cross-sectional regressions of returns on industry concentration are partial, or industry, equilibrium in that they take asgivenconcentration and other covariates that affect returns. Thus, these regressions empirically examine comparative statics on returns with respect to exogenous variations in variables that are (at least temporarily) taken as given. We therefore analyze the implications of tacit collusion and capital investment on the concentration-return relation in this setting.24

Intuitively, industry production capacity plays a central role in tacit collusion because, as seen above, defection incentives are determined by firms‚Äô production capacities both on and off the equilibrium path. In particular, along the equilibrium path, defection incentives are positively related to excess capacity, while these incentives are negatively related to industry capacity in the OEP punishment path. But industry capacity is strongly affected by concentration. Hence, there is a clear link between concentration and risk premium through the effects of the former on defection incentives. 4.1 The role of operating leverage

The analysis in the previous section suggests that operating leverage‚ÄĒdriven by fixed and capacity adjustment costs‚ÄĒwill play an important role in the relative importance of the punishment and market dilution effects. We now formalize this intuition. Consider firms with high fixed-to-marginal costs, resulting in high operating leverage (Lev 1974;Novy-Marx 2011). Such firms will ceteris paribus have low dividends and equity values in lowWt(or high SDF) states. With smooth consumer preferences and production relationships (see Section 1),St=Et[Vt+1]is continuous inWt.25Hence, for everyŌĶts>0,there existsŌĶtw>0such thatSt<ŌĶtswheneverWt<ŌĶtw(holding fixed other elements ofőďt).But because of limited liability,StandS~tare uniformly bounded below by zero. Hence, for operating leverage (OL) sufficiently high andWtsufficiently low, the PE,Et[Vt+1‚ąíV~t+1],can be made arbitrarily close to zero. Consequently, for such firms the MDE must dominate the PE in high SDF states because the markup(pt‚ąíh)stays strictly positive along tacit collusive equilibrium paths. The argument above then implies that ceteris paribusőĒN(Et[Rt+1‚ąíRf,t+1])>0for firms with high OL. An opposing argument applies for firms that have low fixed-to-marginal cost ratios, resulting in low OL or high operating profitability (OP). In this case,Ststays strictly bounded away from zero even in low income states and hence the PE remains significant in high SDF states. 4.3 Time-varying relation of concentration and returns

For analytic tractability, and similar to the common approach in the RBC and asset pricing literatures, we have assumed that the variance-covariance matrix ő£ of innovations to the state variables is stationary. However, a large literature considers time-varying risk premiums. In particular,Duffee (2005)finds that the conditional covariance between aggregate stock returns and consumption growth is high in times of high aggregate stock returns. Because aggregate returns are weighted averages of firm-level returns, this finding would imply that in our setting, on average,‚ąíCovt(ő©t,t+1,Dt+1+St+1St|R^t)<‚ąíCovt(ő©t,t+1,Dt+1+St+1St|Rt),for aggregate returnR^t>Rt.It then follows that|őĒN(Et[Rt+1‚ąíRf,t+1]|R^t)|>|őĒN(Et[Rt+1‚ąíRf,t+1]|Rt)|.Hence,őĒN(Et[Rt+1‚ąíRf,t+1]|R^t)>őĒN(Et[Rt+1‚ąíRf,t+1]|Rt)>0for high OL firms, and this inequality will reverse for high OP and high investment firms (Section 4.1).

4.4 Summary of empirical hypotheses

We now summarize novel empirically testable hypotheses derived in this section, which we will test below. Based on the analysis above (Section 4.1), our main testable hypothesis (Hypothesis 1) focuses on the role of operating leverage (OL) in resolving the ambiguity in the relation of industry concentration and returns. The first part of the hypothesis predicts that the slope of equity returns with respect to concentration is monotone in OL. The second part asserts that the concentration-returns relation becomes negative for sufficiently high values of OL.

Hypothesis 1. Industry concentration is ceteris paribus more negatively related to returns of firms with higher operating leverage and the return-concentration relation is negative for high operating leverage firms.

In a related vein, we also derive from the previous analysis a testable hypothesis on the effect of operating profitability on the concentration-return relation.

Hypothesis 2. Industry concentration is ceteris paribus more positively related to returns of firms with higher operating profitability and the return-concentration relation is positive for high operating profitability firms.

Next, Section 4.2 predicts that industry concentration will be negatively related to risk premium when marginal depreciation costs of capacity utilization,őīňô(u),are high.

Hypothesis 3. Industry concentration is ceteris paribus more negatively related to returns of firms with higher sensitivity of capital depreciation rates to capacity utilization (ie,őīňô(u))‚Ā†.

Furthermore, considering time-varying risk premiums (see Section 4.3) yields the hypotheses that:

Hypothesis 4. The concentration-returns relation as well as the effects of higher operating leverage and operating profitability are ceteris paribus stronger in high aggregate return periods.

Finally, as we noted above, a theoretical literature highlights strategic entry deterrence through capital investment by firms with market power (Spence 1977;Dixit 1980). Consistent with this literature, we can show that, cross-sectionally, capital-intensive high entry-threat industries with higher concentration reflect strong MDE and entry deterrence benefits (seeInternet AppendixG). Meanwhile, a recent literature argues that markups and industry concentration in the United States have increased significantly in recent decades (Autor et¬†al. 2020;De Loecker, Eckhout, and Unger 2020). Evidence indicates that higher concentration and markups are due to a few efficient (‚Äúsuperstar‚ÄĚ) firms (Autor et¬†al. 2020). But increasing market power of superefficient firms will attenuate the importance of larger capacities for entry deterrence in recent years. We thus derive the following hypothesis on the relation of concentration and capital investment.

Hypothesis 5. In capital-intensive high-entry threat industries, capital investment is ceteris paribus positively related to industry concentration. But the relation of industry concentration and capital investment will become weaker in recent years.
article.5 Empirical Tests:
5.1 Data and sample construction

We obtain data on stock returns from CRSP (Center for Research on Security Prices). We construct a number of firm-specific variables, such as size, leverage, using data from Compustat. We employ the text-based TNIC HHI (THHI) industry concentration measure ofHoberg and Phillips (2016), obtained from Gerard Hoberg‚Äôs website.30

Our sample begins in 1989 based on the availability of THHI data. We merge the annual industry concentration measure with the quarterly Compustat files, holding the industry concentration constant over the quarter. To base our empirical tests on the most recent information available to investors, we lag all Compustat data and the industry concentration measure by one quarter. We use CRSP monthly returns and aggregate them to quarterly returns for each firm at the end of March, June, September, and December. We merge this quarterly CRSP file with the quarterly Compustat and the industry concentration data. We also include the data on Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) betas obtained from the WRDS (Wharton Research and Database Services) betasuite. We drop financial firms from our analysis (SIC codes between 6000 and 7000). Further details on sample construction are provided in the Appendix. Our final sample covers 1989-2019, with 8,892 unique firms. We provide details regarding the computation/construction of empirical measures for the dependent and independent variables (used in our tests) in the Appendix.

To test the empirical hypotheses, we run Fama and MacBeth (FM) (1973) cross-sectional regressions using interactions of industry THHI with firm characteristics. article.6 Summary and Conclusions:
Oligopolies are ubiquitous and real and financial outcomes of strategic interaction by firms in such industries attract long-standing, substantial attention. In a dynamic general equilibrium production economy setting with oligopolistic sectors and capital investment, our analysis theoretically, quantitatively and empirically highlights the interaction of firm- and aggregate-level real outcomes with financial markets. In response to productivity shocks, industry firms strategically choose capacity utilization and investment to maximize tacit-collusive equity values subject to a no-defection constraint governed by on- and off-equilibrium equity values, generating substantial interaction between real outcomes and financial markets. The amplified real-financial link also ceteris paribus raises firms‚Äô risk exposure to fundamental shocks. Quantitatively, these effects of tacit collusion are significant, relative to benchmark (ex ante identical) low-concentration industries that follow Bertrand-Nash price competition strategies. Strategic interaction among firms provides novel perspectives on the relation of capital and real frictions‚ÄĒsuch as, fixed costs, capital adjustment costs, and endogenous depreciation‚ÄĒon priced risks. We also find that the relation of industry concentration and equity returns is generally ambiguous depending on the level of operating leverage, reconciling conflicting evidence. We find quantitative and empirical support for novel theoretical predictions regarding the effects of concentration on returns and investment.

Our analysis highlights the significant effects of capital and real frictions on equilibrium outcomes with strategic interaction in dynamic oligopolies. For tractability, we do not consider financial leverage; therefore, the effects of capital and operating leverage we find could reflect the latent impact of leverage. Analyzing tacit collusion in a dynamic production-based asset pricing model with endogenous operational and financial leverage is an important area for future research.

In a similar vein, the multiconsumer goods setting of our model endogenously generates moments and dynamics of the aggregate consumer price index, along with other aggregate variables. For the purposes of our study, the benchmark sector in our quantitative model follows the Bertrand-Nash price competition path and we impose symmetric parameterization across sectors. Extending our quantitative analysis to allow multiple consumer goods sectors with varying degrees of tacit collusion (with associated parameterization) and studying the attendant consequences for firm-level and aggregate real and financial outcomes also deserves attention.

Appendix

A.1 Variable Definitions

Book Equity (BE):We definebook equity=shareholder equity+txditcq‚ÄĒpstkq, wheretxditcqare the (quarterly) deferred taxes and investment tax credit andpstkqis the total preferred stock. We use Compustat variableseqqfor shareholder equity. If it is not available, we compute shareholder equity as the sum of total common equity (ceqq) andpstkq. If this is not available, we compute shareholder equity as the difference between total assets (atq) and total liabilities (ltq). We set negative BE to ‚Äúnot available‚ÄĚ in our tests.

Boom periods:We define boom as periods with quarterly market returns exceeding 10%, which holds for about 15% of our sample‚ÄĒand ‚Äúno boom‚ÄĚ periods (panel B), that is, the 85% of sample periods with quarterly market returns less than 10%.

Capital investment intensity (CII): Defined as the ratio of capital investment (capxq) and the total assets (atq).

Industry concentration:As our industry concentration measure, we utilize the Text-based Network Industry Classifications HHI (THHI) measure ofHoberg and Phillips (2016).

Industry entry threat: InTable¬†7, we identify capital-intensive high entry threat industries through a dummy variable (at the two-digit SIC code level) based on industry-specific entry activity documented inDunne, Roberts, and Samuelson (1988)and capital intensity inKutscher and Mark (1983). These industries include printing, chemicals, business services, research, and engineering and management services.

Leverage:Computed as the ratio of total liabilities (ltq) and the sum of total liabilities and market equity.

Market equity (ME):Computed as the number of shares outstanding times the stock price.

Operating leverage (OL):Computed as the ratio of Selling, General, and Marketing expense (xsgaq) and the sum of Selling, General, and Marketing expense and Cost of Goods sold (cogsq).

Operating profitability (OP):Computed as the ratio: (Total Revenue (REVTQ) - Cost of Goods Sold (cogsq) - Selling, General, and Marketing expense (xsga))/Market Equity (ME).

Stock returns:These are firm-level quarterly total equity returns, measured in percentages.

Tobin‚Äôs q:We follow standard practice in the literature and use the book values of firms‚Äô assets as proxies for their replacement value, and computeqby dividing the sum of market value of equity and total liabilities with the sum of book value of equity and total liabilities.

A.2 Sample Construction

For dependent variables that use Compustat quarterly data, we drop firms with irregular fiscal quarter-ends, that is, with quarter-ends other than March, June, September, and year-end. We then identify firms for which there is a THHI measure. To reduce the likelihood of returns being driven by outliers, we drop extreme observations (e.g., capex/(total assets)) at the 1st and 99th percentiles of their distributions. The sample selection procedure results in 8,892 unique firms.
